_meta.authoringNotes: Citations verified against Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guidelines on Acromegaly (2014), Prolactinoma (2011), Cushing Syndrome Treatment (2015), Hypopituitarism (2016), Pituitary Apoplexy (UK Pituitary Society 2011), and Melmed NEJM 2020 Pituitary review (PMID 31794889).

h1Title: Pituitary Disorders: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Pituitary disorders are diseases of the master endocrine gland at the base of the brain that either oversecrete or undersecrete one or more hormones, or compress adjacent structures by mass effect. Pituitary adenomas are common — incidental detection on MRI reaches roughly 10% of adults, while clinically significant disease affects about 1 in 1,000 people. Functional tumors include prolactinomas (50-60% of clinical adenomas), growth hormone-secreting (acromegaly), ACTH-secreting (Cushing disease), and rare TSH-secreting tumors. Non-functional adenomas, hypopituitarism, diabetes insipidus, and pituitary apoplexy round out the field. Presentations span menstrual irregularity, infertility, visual field loss, headache, growth changes, weight shifts, and crisis-level cortisol deficiency. Modern care — driven by transsphenoidal endoscopic surgery, dopamine agonists, somatostatin analogs, and stereotactic radiosurgery — controls most disease and preserves life expectancy when treated promptly.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidental pituitary lesions on MRI in roughly 10% of adults; clinically significant adenomas in roughly 1 in 1,000
keyStats.demographics: Prolactinomas predominate in women aged 20-50; acromegaly and Cushing disease are equally distributed
keyStats.avgAge: Most adenomas present age 30-60; pituitary apoplexy median age 50-60
keyStats.globalCases: No single global estimate; population-based studies suggest 80-100 cases per 100,000 adults

definition: Pituitary disorders (ICD-10: E22, E23, E24) comprise diseases of the anterior and posterior pituitary gland that disrupt the regulated secretion of pituitary hormones — growth hormone, prolactin, adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and antidiuretic hormone (ADH or vasopressin). The disorders span six broad clinical entities: pituitary adenomas (benign neoplasms classified by hormone secreted and by size — microadenomas <10 mm, macroadenomas ≥10 mm), hypopituitarism (deficiency of one or more anterior pituitary hormones), diabetes insipidus (ADH deficiency from posterior pituitary or hypothalamic damage), pituitary apoplexy (acute hemorrhage or infarction of the gland), syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH), and empty sella syndrome. Functional adenomas overproduce specific hormones: prolactinoma (50-60% of clinical adenomas), growth hormone-secreting adenoma causing acromegaly, ACTH-secreting adenoma causing Cushing disease, and rare TSH-secreting adenoma. Non-functional adenomas produce no clinical hormone excess but cause symptoms through mass effect on optic chiasm, cranial nerves, or surrounding pituitary tissue. Care is led by endocrinologists in partnership with neurosurgeons (transsphenoidal endoscopic specialists), radiation oncologists, neuro-ophthalmologists, and neuroradiologists at pituitary centers of excellence.

typesClassification.description: Prolactin-secreting adenoma; 50-60% of clinical pituitary tumors. Microprolactinomas more common in women (menstrual disturbance, galactorrhea, infertility); macroprolactinomas more common in men (mass effect, hypogonadism). First-line treatment is medical (cabergoline).

Excess growth hormone and IGF-1 in adults causes facial coarsening, enlarged extremities, deepened voice, sleep apnea, diabetes, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy. Untreated, doubles cardiovascular mortality. First-line treatment is transsphenoidal surgery.

Excess ACTH from a pituitary corticotroph adenoma drives adrenal cortisol oversecretion. Presents with central weight gain, thin skin, easy bruising, hypertension, glucose intolerance, and proximal myopathy. First-line treatment is transsphenoidal surgery.

Produces no clinical hormone excess. Symptoms arise from mass effect: bitemporal hemianopia from optic chiasm compression, headache, and hypopituitarism from pressure on residual gland. Surgery indicated for visual or chiasmal compression.

Deficiency of one or more anterior pituitary hormones. Causes include adenomas, surgery, radiation, traumatic brain injury, infiltrative disease, and Sheehan syndrome (post-partum pituitary necrosis). Replacement hormones (hydrocortisone, levothyroxine, sex hormones, growth hormone) are prescribed for missing axes.

ADH deficiency from posterior pituitary or hypothalamic damage produces polyuria (>3 L/day) and dilute urine despite hypernatremia. Confirmed by water-deprivation testing or copeptin levels. Treated with desmopressin.

Acute hemorrhage or infarction of a pituitary tumor or gland producing sudden severe headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, and adrenal crisis. Endocrine emergency requiring immediate stress-dose hydrocortisone.

primarySymptoms: Persistent headache, often retroorbital or bitemporal, that does not respond to typical analgesics and may worsen with rising intracranial pressure.
Visual disturbance — most classically bitemporal hemianopia (loss of the outer half of each visual field) from optic chiasm compression by a macroadenoma.
Menstrual irregularity, amenorrhea, infertility, or galactorrhea (milk production unrelated to childbirth) in women with prolactinoma or any cause of hyperprolactinemia.
Loss of libido, erectile dysfunction, infertility, or reduced muscle mass in men, reflecting hyperprolactinemia or secondary hypogonadism.
Growth of hands, feet, and facial features (jaw, nose, supraorbital ridge) over years in untreated acromegaly, often with rings or shoes no longer fitting.
Central weight gain with thin extremities, round red face, easy bruising, purple abdominal striae, hypertension, and proximal muscle weakness in Cushing disease.
Excessive thirst (polydipsia) and dilute urination over 3 liters daily in central diabetes insipidus, often disrupting sleep.
Fatigue, cold intolerance, hair loss, dry skin, and weight gain reflecting secondary hypothyroidism from TSH deficiency.
Postural dizziness, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, nausea, and pigmentation changes pointing to secondary adrenal insufficiency from ACTH deficiency.
Sudden severe thunderclap headache with visual loss and double vision in pituitary apoplexy — a neurosurgical and endocrine emergency.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent monthly menstrual irregularity or infertility with elevated prolactin on first-line endocrine screen
Subtle facial change, enlarging shoe or ring size, or new sleep apnea in a middle-aged adult — early acromegaly
Treatment-resistant hypertension with hypokalemia, thin skin, or unexplained osteoporosis — consider Cushing syndrome
Unexplained fatigue, postural dizziness, and hyponatremia — possible secondary adrenal insufficiency
Unexplained polyuria of dilute urine in a patient with prior head injury, pituitary surgery, or radiation
emergencySigns: Sudden severe (thunderclap) headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia, or altered consciousness — pituitary apoplexy with adrenal crisis risk; treat with immediate hydrocortisone and urgent MRI
Acute hyponatremia (sodium <125 mmol/L) with seizure or altered mental status — SIADH or adrenal crisis from undiagnosed hypopituitarism
Hypernatremia, hypotension, and dehydration in known or suspected diabetes insipidus — urgent fluid replacement and desmopressin
New-onset diplopia or cranial nerve palsy in a patient with known pituitary mass — possible apoplexy or rapid expansion
Severe headache after head injury followed by polyuria — post-traumatic central diabetes insipidus

causes.description: The dominant cause of clinically significant pituitary disease. Most adenomas arise sporadically from somatic mutations such as GNAS in growth hormone-secreting tumors and USP8 in corticotroph adenomas, with no identifiable inherited cause.

MEN1 (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1) accounts for roughly 3% of pituitary adenomas and clusters with parathyroid and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. AIP-related familial isolated pituitary adenomas, Carney complex, and McCune-Albright syndrome are rarer hereditary causes.

Transsphenoidal surgery, pituitary radiotherapy, and cranial radiation for head and neck or brain tumors all damage pituitary tissue progressively. Hypopituitarism develops in 30-50% of patients within 5-10 years of radiotherapy.

Moderate-to-severe traumatic brain injury causes hypopituitarism in 25-40% of survivors. Mechanism includes direct hypothalamic-pituitary injury and ischemia. Subarachnoid hemorrhage and pituitary stalk injury similarly cause acute or delayed deficiencies.

Acute hemorrhage or infarction of a pituitary adenoma or normal gland. Often triggered by pregnancy, anticoagulation, hypertension, surgery, or dynamic endocrine testing. Sheehan syndrome is the obstetric variant of pituitary necrosis from postpartum hemorrhage.

Lymphocytic hypophysitis (often peripartum or related to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy), sarcoidosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis, hemochromatosis, tuberculosis, and IgG4-related disease can infiltrate the pituitary and cause panhypopituitarism or diabetes insipidus.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of pituitary adenoma or MEN syndromes
riskFactors.description: MEN1, AIP, Carney complex, and McCune-Albright carriers face elevated risk. Screening offered to first-degree relatives in affected families.
Cranial irradiation for childhood leukemia, brain tumors, or head and neck cancers raises lifetime risk of pituitary adenoma and hypopituitarism. Latencies range from 5 to 30 years.
Moderate-to-severe TBI causes hypopituitarism in 25-40% of survivors. Screening at 3-6 months and 12 months post-injury is recommended.

Significant peripartum hypotension can produce ischemic pituitary necrosis (Sheehan syndrome) and progressive hypopituitarism. Now uncommon in high-income countries; remains a leading cause in low-resource settings.
Ipilimumab and other checkpoint inhibitors cause autoimmune hypophysitis in 10-15% of treated patients. Most experience permanent ACTH or TSH deficiency requiring lifelong replacement.
Pregnancy stimulates physiologic pituitary enlargement and can trigger apoplexy in pre-existing adenomas, especially prolactinomas and macroadenomas.
Anticoagulant therapy raises the risk of hemorrhage in pre-existing pituitary tumors, sometimes precipitating apoplexy.

affectedDemographics: Pituitary adenomas account for 10-15% of intracranial neoplasms — third most common after meningiomas and gliomas
Prolactinomas: 5-10x more common in women aged 20-50; men more often present with macroadenomas due to delayed diagnosis
Acromegaly: equal sex distribution; median age at diagnosis 40-50; diagnostic delay 5-10 years typical
Cushing disease: 3-5x more common in women; peak age 25-40
Sheehan syndrome remains a leading cause of hypopituitarism in low- and middle-income countries with limited obstetric services
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pituitary disorders combines clinical recognition, biochemical confirmation, and pituitary MRI. The workup begins by identifying which axis is affected. For suspected hyperprolactinemia, fasting serum prolactin above 200 ng/mL strongly suggests prolactinoma; modest elevations (25-100 ng/mL) require rule-out of pregnancy, hypothyroidism, renal failure, and dopamine-antagonist medications. For acromegaly, the Endocrine Society 2014 guideline recommends serum IGF-1 measurement first; a clearly elevated IGF-1 plus failure of growth hormone to suppress below 1 ng/mL during a 75-gram oral glucose tolerance test is diagnostic. For Cushing syndrome, screening uses 24-hour urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, or low-dose dexamethasone suppression — two abnormal results from different tests confirm the diagnosis. ACTH measurement then distinguishes ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic source) from ACTH-independent (adrenal) Cushing. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling localizes a pituitary source when MRI is non-revealing. For hypopituitarism, basal hormone testing (cortisol, free T4, TSH, prolactin, IGF-1, LH, FSH, sex hormones, plasma osmolality) is followed by stimulation tests (insulin tolerance test, glucagon stimulation, or short Synacthen) as needed. Diabetes insipidus is diagnosed with water-deprivation testing or copeptin measurement. Pituitary MRI with dedicated thin-slice gadolinium-enhanced protocol is the imaging gold standard, detecting microadenomas down to 3-5 mm. Formal visual field testing (Humphrey perimetry) is mandatory whenever a macroadenoma is identified. In suspected pituitary apoplexy, urgent MRI (or CT if MRI unavailable) plus immediate stress-dose hydrocortisone are the first steps.

diagnosticTests.test: Pituitary MRI with thin-slice gadolinium-enhanced protocol
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging for pituitary lesions. Detects microadenomas down to 3-5 mm, defines macroadenoma extension to cavernous sinus or optic chiasm, and identifies apoplectic hemorrhage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard MRI lasting 30-45 minutes with intravenous gadolinium contrast. Lying still in a narrow tube is required; sedation is available for claustrophobic patients.

diagnosticTests.test: Basal anterior pituitary hormone panel (cortisol, ACTH, TSH, free T4, prolactin, IGF-1, LH, FSH, testosterone or estradiol)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for hyperfunction or hypofunction in each anterior pituitary axis. First-line biochemistry for any suspected pituitary disorder.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard morning blood draws (8-9 AM for cortisol and ACTH). Results return within 1-3 days for most analytes; IGF-1 may take longer in some labs.

diagnosticTests.test: Oral glucose tolerance test with growth hormone measurement
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms acromegaly when IGF-1 is elevated. Failure of growth hormone to suppress below 1 ng/mL after 75 g oral glucose confirms autonomous growth hormone secretion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting baseline blood draws, a 75-gram glucose drink, and serial blood samples over 2 hours. Mild nausea after the glucose drink is common.

diagnosticTests.test: Low-dose dexamethasone suppression and late-night salivary cortisol
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for Cushing syndrome. Two abnormal results from different tests are required to confirm. Inferior petrosal sinus sampling is added if ACTH-dependent Cushing is established but MRI is non-revealing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dexamethasone tablets are taken at 11 PM; cortisol is measured at 8 AM the next morning. Salivary samples are collected at home with provided kits.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms central diabetes insipidus and distinguishes it from primary polydipsia and nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Copeptin has largely replaced classic water deprivation in many centers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Water-deprivation testing requires inpatient monitoring of urine and plasma osmolality over 6-8 hours. Copeptin is a single blood sample after a hypertonic saline infusion or arginine stimulation, performed in specialist centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory whenever a macroadenoma is identified or visual symptoms are reported. Detects bitemporal hemianopia and follows changes after treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 minute test in an ophthalmology clinic. The patient sits at a perimeter and presses a button when seeing flashing lights at various positions.

diagnosticTests.test: Stimulation testing for hypopituitarism (insulin tolerance, glucagon stimulation, short Synacthen)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms adrenal and growth hormone deficiencies when basal levels are inconclusive. Insulin tolerance testing remains the gold standard but requires careful monitoring.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in an endocrinology day unit. Insulin is given intravenously to induce mild hypoglycemia, with serial cortisol and growth hormone sampling. Glucagon stimulation is a safer alternative.

treatmentOverview: Treatment depends on tumor type, size, hormonal activity, and symptoms. Prolactinomas are the exception to the surgery-first rule — first-line treatment is dopamine agonists, particularly cabergoline 0.25-1 mg twice weekly, which normalizes prolactin in 80-90% of microprolactinomas and shrinks macroprolactinomas in 80%. Bromocriptine remains an alternative, especially in pregnancy. Acromegaly requires transsphenoidal surgery as first-line when feasible, with surgical remission rates of 80-90% for microadenomas and 50-60% for macroadenomas. Somatostatin receptor ligands (octreotide LAR, lanreotide, pasireotide) normalize IGF-1 in 50-60% of patients who fail or are unfit for surgery. The growth hormone receptor antagonist pegvisomant adds another option (normalizes IGF-1 in 60-70%). Cushing disease is also treated with transsphenoidal surgery as first-line, with remission rates of 70-80% for microadenomas. Failed or recurrent disease may require repeat surgery, stereotactic radiosurgery, medical therapy with steroidogenesis inhibitors (ketoconazole, metyrapone, osilodrostat), or rarely bilateral adrenalectomy. Non-functional macroadenomas are operated when visual or chiasmal compression is present, when there is significant hypopituitarism, or when serial imaging shows growth. Microadenomas without endocrine excess or compression are observed. Hypopituitarism requires lifelong replacement of deficient hormones: hydrocortisone (15-25 mg daily in divided doses) for ACTH deficiency, levothyroxine for TSH deficiency, sex steroids (estrogen-progesterone or testosterone) for gonadotropin deficiency, growth hormone (selected adults), and desmopressin for diabetes insipidus. Stereotactic radiosurgery is reserved for residual or recurrent tumors after surgery. Pituitary apoplexy is a medical emergency: immediate intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg, urgent neurosurgical assessment, and decompression if visual deficits do not improve over hours. Multidisciplinary review at a pituitary center of excellence is associated with better outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Cabergoline (0.25-1 mg twice weekly) and bromocriptine (2.5-15 mg daily) — dopamine agonists
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for prolactinoma at any size. Dopamine agonists suppress prolactin secretion and induce tumor shrinkage. Cabergoline is better tolerated than bromocriptine; bromocriptine is preferred in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cabergoline normalizes prolactin in 80-90% of microprolactinomas and 70-80% of macroprolactinomas; tumor shrinkage in 80% of macroprolactinomas.

medicalTreatments.name: Somatostatin receptor ligands (octreotide LAR 20-30 mg monthly IM, lanreotide 60-120 mg monthly SC, pasireotide 40-60 mg monthly)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in acromegaly for residual or persistent disease after surgery, and in selected non-surgical candidates. Pasireotide also approved for Cushing disease unresponsive to surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes IGF-1 in 50-60% of acromegaly patients; reduces tumor size in 40-50%. Pasireotide normalizes urinary free cortisol in 40-60% of Cushing disease cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Pegvisomant (10-30 mg daily SC) — growth hormone receptor antagonist
medicalTreatments.description: Used in acromegaly when somatostatin analogs do not control IGF-1. Blocks peripheral growth hormone action rather than secretion. Requires liver function monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes IGF-1 in 60-70% of patients refractory to surgery and somatostatin analogs.

medicalTreatments.name: Steroidogenesis inhibitors (ketoconazole 200-1200 mg daily, metyrapone 0.5-6 g daily, osilodrostat 2-30 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Block adrenal cortisol synthesis in Cushing syndrome that does not respond to surgery or as bridge therapy. Osilodrostat is the most potent recent addition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Osilodrostat normalizes urinary free cortisol in 70-80% of treated patients; metyrapone and ketoconazole 50-70%.

medicalTreatments.name: Desmopressin (DDAVP, 100-400 µg orally or 10-40 µg intranasally daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Synthetic ADH analog used in central diabetes insipidus. Restores urine concentration and prevents dehydration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores normal urine output in over 95% of central diabetes insipidus patients when titrated correctly; main risk is hyponatremia from overdose.

medicalTreatments.name: Glucocorticoid replacement (hydrocortisone 15-25 mg daily in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Mandatory in ACTH-deficient hypopituitarism. Doses are doubled or tripled during illness, infection, or surgery to prevent adrenal crisis. Patients carry an emergency steroid card and parenteral hydrocortisone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores adequate cortisol exposure in nearly all patients when titrated; underdosing causes fatigue and orthostatic hypotension, overdosing causes weight gain and osteoporosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Growth hormone replacement (somatropin 0.2-0.6 mg daily SC)
medicalTreatments.description: Considered in adult growth hormone deficiency with quality-of-life impairment and adverse metabolic profile. Titrated to age and sex-adjusted IGF-1 levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves body composition, exercise capacity, lipid profile, and quality of life; long-term cardiovascular benefit suggested but not proven.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic transsphenoidal surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Standard surgical approach for most pituitary tumors. The surgeon accesses the gland through the nose and sphenoid sinus using a high-definition endoscope. Performed at high-volume pituitary centers.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Remission rates 70-90% for microadenomas (Cushing, acromegaly) and 50-60% for macroadenomas at experienced centers.

surgicalOptions.name: Transcranial pituitary surgery (rare)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for large tumors with significant suprasellar extension that cannot be adequately decompressed transsphenoidally. Increasing rarity as endoscopic techniques have advanced.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Comparable visual outcomes to transsphenoidal in selected cases; higher morbidity.

surgicalOptions.name: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife)
surgicalOptions.description: Single-fraction high-dose radiation to residual or recurrent pituitary tumor after surgery. Endocrine remission develops slowly over 2-5 years.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Tumor control in 90% at 5 years; endocrine remission in 30-50% for functional tumors. Long-term hypopituitarism in 30-50% of treated patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Conventional fractionated radiotherapy
surgicalOptions.description: Used when stereotactic radiosurgery is not feasible because of proximity to optic apparatus. Slower onset of effect than radiosurgery; higher rates of late hypopituitarism.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Tumor control in 90% at 10 years; hypopituitarism in 50-80% at 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Bilateral adrenalectomy (refractory Cushing)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for severe refractory Cushing disease when surgery and medical therapy fail. Permanently abolishes adrenal cortisol production but commits the patient to lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement and the risk of Nelson syndrome.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Immediate biochemical remission in nearly all patients; Nelson syndrome (corticotroph tumor expansion) in 25-50% over years.

whySeeSpecialist: Pituitary disorders require coordinated care at a pituitary center of excellence because diagnosis spans seven hormone axes and imaging, treatment combines surgery, medication, and radiotherapy with significant volume-outcome relationships, and incidental pituitary lesions often need observation rather than intervention. Endocrinologists, transsphenoidal neurosurgeons, neuro-ophthalmologists, neuroradiologists, and radiation oncologists should review complex cases jointly.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an endocrinologist subspecialized in pituitary disease, partnered with a neurosurgeon performing at least 50 transsphenoidal pituitary operations annually (volume strongly correlates with remission rates), institutional access to stereotactic radiosurgery, on-site neuro-ophthalmology, and active multidisciplinary pituitary tumor boards. Pituitary centers of excellence are formally accredited in several countries.
preventionStrategies: No effective primary prevention exists for sporadic pituitary tumors; screening focuses on early detection in at-risk groups
Annual biochemical screening in known MEN1 carriers and AIP-mutation families from adolescence
Post-cranial-radiation hypopituitarism surveillance with annual pituitary hormone panel for 10+ years
Post-traumatic-brain-injury pituitary screening at 3 months and 12 months in moderate-to-severe TBI survivors
Education for women on postpartum hemorrhage warning signs to detect Sheehan syndrome early
Pituitary axis monitoring during and after immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy in oncology patients
lifestyleModifications: Carry an emergency steroid card and parenteral hydrocortisone when on glucocorticoid replacement
Notify all clinicians of hypopituitarism status before surgery, dental work, or infection — stress-dose adjustment is essential
Track fluid intake and urine output if on desmopressin to avoid hyponatremia
Engage in regular exercise to support bone density, particularly with chronic glucocorticoid use
Maintain dietary salt intake within recommendations to support blood pressure on glucocorticoid replacement
Schedule lifelong endocrinology follow-up — most pituitary conditions require multi-decade monitoring

recommended: Balanced Mediterranean-style diet to support cardiovascular health in acromegaly and Cushing disease
Adequate calcium (1000-1200 mg daily) and vitamin D (800-2000 IU daily) on glucocorticoid replacement to protect bone
Lean protein, vegetables, and whole grains to manage weight and metabolic syndrome in Cushing disease
Hydration tailored to desmopressin use in diabetes insipidus — avoid both dehydration and water overload
Iodized salt in hypothyroidism on levothyroxine replacement
avoid: High-sodium and processed foods if hypertension complicates acromegaly or Cushing disease
Excess alcohol — interferes with cortisol metabolism and worsens osteoporosis on glucocorticoids
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice on ketoconazole or osilodrostat (CYP3A4 interactions)
Crash diets and prolonged fasting on hydrocortisone — risks adrenal crisis
Excess fluid intake on desmopressin — risks hyponatremia

exerciseGuidelines: Most patients with treated pituitary disorders can exercise normally. Glucocorticoid-replacement patients should increase doses on heavy training or competition days. Acromegaly patients with cardiomyopathy or sleep apnea need clearance before high-intensity training. Patients with macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm should avoid Valsalva-heavy resistance training preoperatively.
dailyManagement: Take all hormone replacements at consistent times daily — adherence underpins long-term outcomes
Carry an emergency steroid identification card and stress-dose hydrocortisone if ACTH-deficient
Monitor and report any new or worsening symptoms — headache, visual change, fatigue, weight shift
Attend scheduled imaging and biochemical follow-up appointments
Educate close contacts on how to administer emergency hydrocortisone in adrenal crisis
Wear a medical alert bracelet identifying hypopituitarism or diabetes insipidus
prognosis: Prognosis varies sharply by tumor type and treatment access. Prolactinomas have excellent prognosis: cabergoline normalizes prolactin in 80-90%, fertility is preserved, and long-term mortality is unchanged from the general population. Acromegaly treated to biochemical remission has near-normal life expectancy; untreated acromegaly carries doubled cardiovascular mortality. Cushing disease in remission has approximately normal survival; persistent active Cushing carries 4-5 fold mortality from cardiovascular and infectious complications. Non-functional macroadenomas have favorable prognosis when treated promptly for visual or chiasmal compression; visual recovery is partial in 80-90% of patients operated within months of symptom onset. Hypopituitarism, with optimal replacement, allows essentially normal life expectancy and quality of life, though long-term cardiovascular and skeletal risks remain modestly elevated. Pituitary apoplexy survival exceeds 90% with prompt steroid replacement and decompression when indicated. The decisive prognostic factors are early diagnosis, biochemical remission, and lifelong adherence to replacement and surveillance.
recoveryTimeline: Transsphenoidal surgery typically requires 2-3 days in hospital and 4-6 weeks for full activity. Visual fields begin to improve within days of decompression and continue to recover for 12 months. Biochemical remission is confirmed by hormone testing at 6 weeks. Cabergoline-treated prolactinomas show prolactin normalization in weeks and tumor shrinkage over 3-6 months. Radiotherapy effects unfold over 2-5 years for biochemical remission. Hypopituitarism replacement requires titration over 4-8 weeks to optimal symptom control. Diabetes insipidus responds within hours to desmopressin.
complications: Pituitary apoplexy with sudden visual loss and adrenal crisis — endocrine and neurosurgical emergency
Adrenal crisis from undertreatment of ACTH deficiency — life-threatening shock during illness or stress
Cardiomyopathy, sleep apnea, hypertension, diabetes, and colon polyps in poorly controlled acromegaly
Cardiovascular, infectious, and thromboembolic complications of active Cushing syndrome
Hyponatremia from desmopressin overdose or SIADH — encephalopathy and seizure risk
Osteoporosis with chronic glucocorticoid replacement or persistent hypogonadism
Postoperative cerebrospinal fluid leak, meningitis, or new hypopituitarism after pituitary surgery

supportResources.name: Pituitary Foundation (UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing helplines, education materials, and support for people with all pituitary disorders.

supportResources.name: Pituitary Network Association (US)
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organization offering education, support groups, and clinical center directories.

supportResources.name: Endocrine Society Hormone Health Network
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources covering pituitary, adrenal, thyroid, and other endocrine disorders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thyroiditis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Thyroiditis affects the thyroid gland directly and presents with neck pain or swelling, abnormal thyroid function, and positive thyroid autoantibodies. Pituitary disorders affecting the thyroid axis cause central hypothyroidism (low free T4 with inappropriately normal or low TSH) without primary thyroid pathology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thyrotoxicosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Thyrotoxicosis is the clinical state of excess thyroid hormone — usually from primary thyroid disease such as Graves disease or toxic nodule. Rare TSH-secreting pituitary adenomas cause central thyrotoxicosis with elevated free T4 and inappropriately normal or high TSH. Distinguished by TSH receptor antibodies, thyroid scan, and pituitary MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Goiter
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Goiter is enlargement of the thyroid gland, usually visible and palpable in the neck. Pituitary disorders do not produce a primary goiter; central hypothyroidism from TSH deficiency typically presents with a normal or small thyroid.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetes Insipidus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetes insipidus is a specific pituitary or kidney disorder of ADH signaling producing polyuria and dilute urine. It is one type of pituitary disorder (central form) and is distinguished from type 2 diabetes by normal blood glucose and from primary polydipsia by water-deprivation testing or copeptin.

relatedConditions.name: Thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve hormone-secreting glands; central versus primary thyroid disorders distinguished by paired TSH and free T4.

relatedConditions.name: Thyrotoxicosis
relatedConditions.relevance: TSH-secreting pituitary adenomas are a rare cause of thyrotoxicosis; biochemical pattern differs from primary thyroid hyperfunction.

relatedConditions.name: Goiter
relatedConditions.relevance: Primary thyroid pathology; pituitary disorders rarely cause goiter and are distinguished by central versus primary thyroid hormone patterns.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Central (secondary) hypothyroidism arises from pituitary or hypothalamic disease and is a frequent manifestation of hypopituitarism.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetes Insipidus
relatedConditions.relevance: Central diabetes insipidus is a posterior pituitary disorder; nephrogenic diabetes insipidus is a kidney disorder of ADH resistance.

faqs.question: What is a pituitary disorder?
faqs.answer: Pituitary disorders are diseases of the master endocrine gland at the base of the brain.

faqs.question: What is the most common pituitary tumor?
faqs.answer: Prolactinoma is the most common functional pituitary adenoma, accounting for 50-60% of clinical cases. Non-functional adenomas account for roughly 30%. Growth hormone-secreting tumors (acromegaly), ACTH-secreting tumors (Cushing disease), and rare TSH-secreting tumors make up the rest.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of a pituitary tumor?
faqs.answer: Symptoms depend on the tumor type. Hormone excess produces specific syndromes: menstrual disturbance and galactorrhea in prolactinoma, facial coarsening and enlarging hands in acromegaly, central weight gain and easy bruising in Cushing disease.

faqs.question: How is a pituitary disorder diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines hormonal blood tests, dynamic stimulation or suppression tests, and pituitary MRI with thin-slice gadolinium-enhanced protocol. Visual field testing is mandatory whenever a macroadenoma is identified.

faqs.question: Can pituitary tumors be cured?
faqs.answer: Many can. Microprolactinomas are controlled with cabergoline in 80-90% of patients. Acromegaly and Cushing disease microadenomas are cured by transsphenoidal surgery in 70-90% of cases at experienced centers.

faqs.question: What is hypopituitarism?
faqs.answer: Hypopituitarism is deficiency of one or more anterior pituitary hormones. Causes include pituitary adenoma, surgery, radiation, traumatic brain injury, autoimmune hypophysitis, and Sheehan syndrome after postpartum hemorrhage.

faqs.question: What is acromegaly?
faqs.answer: Acromegaly is excess growth hormone secretion in adulthood, almost always from a pituitary adenoma. It produces gradual facial coarsening, enlarged hands and feet, deepened voice, sleep apnea, diabetes, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy.

faqs.question: What is Cushing disease?
faqs.answer: Cushing disease is excess ACTH secretion from a pituitary corticotroph adenoma, which drives adrenal cortisol overproduction. Presents with central weight gain, thin skin, easy bruising, hypertension, glucose intolerance, and proximal myopathy.

faqs.question: What is a prolactinoma?
faqs.answer: Prolactinoma is the most common pituitary tumor, secreting excess prolactin. Microprolactinomas (<10 mm) typically present in women with amenorrhea, galactorrhea, or infertility. Macroprolactinomas (≥10 mm) cause headache, visual field loss, and hypogonadism, particularly in men.

faqs.question: Is pituitary surgery dangerous?
faqs.answer: Endoscopic transsphenoidal pituitary surgery at high-volume centers carries low morbidity. Major complications including cerebrospinal fluid leak, meningitis, new permanent hypopituitarism, and visual deterioration occur in under 5% combined.

faqs.question: What is pituitary apoplexy?
faqs.answer: Pituitary apoplexy is sudden hemorrhage or infarction in a pituitary adenoma or normal gland, producing thunderclap headache, visual loss, eye movement paralysis, and adrenal crisis.

faqs.question: What is central diabetes insipidus?
faqs.answer: Central diabetes insipidus is deficiency of antidiuretic hormone (ADH) from posterior pituitary or hypothalamic damage. It produces large volumes of dilute urine (>3 L daily), excessive thirst, and risk of dehydration and hypernatremia. Diagnosis uses water-deprivation testing or copeptin.

faqs.question: Can pituitary tumors be inherited?
faqs.answer: Roughly 5% of pituitary adenomas are inherited. MEN1 (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1) accounts for 3%; AIP-related familial isolated pituitary adenomas, Carney complex, and McCune-Albright syndrome are rarer.

faqs.question: What is Sheehan syndrome?
faqs.answer: Sheehan syndrome is hypopituitarism caused by ischemic pituitary necrosis after severe postpartum hemorrhage. Women present with failure to lactate, prolonged amenorrhea, fatigue, weight loss, and hyponatremia in the months to years after delivery. Lifelong hormone replacement is required.

faqs.question: Will I need lifelong medication for a pituitary disorder?
faqs.answer: Depends on the disorder. Hypopituitarism requires lifelong replacement of deficient hormones. Cured pituitary adenomas (e.g., acromegaly after successful surgery) may not need long-term medication but require lifelong surveillance for recurrence.

faqs.question: How often should pituitary tumors be monitored?
faqs.answer: Microadenomas and incidental small lesions are typically monitored with MRI and hormone testing every 6-12 months initially, then less frequently if stable. Macroadenomas and post-treatment tumors require more intensive follow-up. Lifelong surveillance is recommended for all functional adenomas and for hypopituitarism.

faqs.question: Can pituitary disorders cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hyperprolactinemia, hypopituitarism with gonadotropin deficiency, and acromegaly all impair fertility. Most causes are treatable: cabergoline restores fertility in 80-90% of women with prolactinoma; gonadotropin replacement (LH and FSH) restores fertility in selected hypopituitary patients. Pre-conception counseling at a pituitary center is recommended.

faqs.question: Is radiation therapy used for pituitary tumors?
faqs.answer: Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife) is used for residual or recurrent tumors after surgery and for tumors not amenable to operation. Conventional fractionated radiotherapy is reserved for tumors close to the optic apparatus. Both achieve tumor control in roughly 90% at 5-10 years but cause delayed hypopituitarism in 30-80% of treated patients.

faqs.question: How much does pituitary disease treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Cabergoline is inexpensive in most countries. Transsphenoidal surgery at a high-volume US center ranges from USD 30,000-60,000 with insurance coverage typical. Long-acting somatostatin analogs cost USD 20,000-50,000 annually. Pasireotide and pegvisomant are among the most expensive endocrine medications. In India and many emerging markets, generic cabergoline is widely affordable.

faqs.question: When should I see an endocrinologist for a pituitary concern?
faqs.answer: See an endocrinologist for unexplained menstrual irregularity with elevated prolactin, suspected acromegaly or Cushing features, treatment-resistant hypertension with hypokalemia, polyuria with dilute urine, persistent fatigue with low cortisol, or any incidental pituitary lesion found on imaging. Pituitary centers of excellence offer coordinated diagnosis and treatment.
